Paul opens Romans with a declaration of his entire commission: he is set apart for the gospel of God. Notice what he emphasizes about this gospel. It is not new. It was promised beforehand through the prophets in the Holy Scriptures. It concerns the Son of God—who is both a true descendant of David according to the flesh and was declared with power to be the Son of God through His resurrection. This is the gospel Paul will unfold: that Jesus Christ is Lord, eternally the Son, truly human, and vindicated by His rising from the dead. Everything that follows in Romans flows from this foundation. The gospel is not Paul's invention or his improvement on an older message. It is God's ancient purpose now revealed in the person and work of Christ. When Paul says he is "set apart" for this gospel, he means his entire existence has been reoriented around proclaiming this reality to the Gentiles. His longing to see the Roman church, his constant prayer, his willingness to go anywhere—all of it derives from the weight of what he has been given to announce.

But then Paul declares something startling: "I am not ashamed of the gospel." Why would he need to say this? Because the gospel was a stumbling block. A crucified Messiah was nonsense to Greek philosophers. The message that salvation comes through faith in a executed Jewish teacher contradicted the wisdom of the age. Paul knew the cost of this message. Yet he speaks of it as "the power of God for salvation"—not in spite of its weakness, but through it. The gospel does what no human argument or achievement can do: it saves. It saves the Jew first, then the Greek, because it saves everyone who believes. And it does this by revealing the righteousness of God that comes by faith from start to finish. What Paul means is that God's entire saving work, from beginning to end, operates on the principle of faith. The righteous will live by faith—not by works, not by status, not by human wisdom, but by trusting God.

Then Paul pivots to show why humanity needs this gospel so desperately. From verse 18 onward, he unveils the wrath of God—not as arbitrary anger, but as His judicial response to willful rebellion. What Paul describes is not ignorance but suppression. The truth about God is not hidden. It is "plain" because "God has made it plain." Creation itself testifies to His invisible qualities, His eternal power, His divine nature. Anyone with eyes can see the workmanship and understand what it reveals about the Creator. Yet humanity does not glorify Him as God. They do not give thanks. Instead, they exchange the glory of the immortal God for images of mortal creatures. They worship the creation rather than the Creator. And God, in His justice, gives them over to their desires. He withdraws His restraint. He abandons them to the very lusts they have chosen. This is not arbitrary punishment; it is the logical consequence of rejecting the only source of true life. The escalating list of sins in verses 29-31—murder, envy, deceit, malice, gossip, slander, God-hatred—shows the trajectory of a mind separated from God. And the final word is devastating: they not only continue in these things but also "approve of those who practice them." They have inverted the moral order itself.

Here is what confronts you in this chapter: You live in a world where the truth about God is plain, yet it is constantly suppressed. And you yourself have been given grace through Christ to see what so many do not. The gospel that saves you—that declares Christ as Lord, that calls you to faith, that offers you righteousness—is not a private comfort. It is the power of God. Paul's passion for the Roman church, his willingness to endure all things, his refusal to be ashamed—these flow from understanding the gospel's true magnitude. The question this chapter presses on you is not whether you believe in God generally, but whether you will glorify Him as God, give Him thanks, and live by faith in the One He has sent. Will you simply know about God, or will you glorify and trust Him?
